name: Ad Hominem
description: Attacking the person making an argument rather than the argument itself. The truth of a claim is independent of the character or motives of the person asserting it.
example: You can't trust John's argument about climate change — he drives a gas-powered car.
structure: Person P makes claim C. Person P has flaw F. Therefore C is false.

name: Straw Man
description: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument by exaggerating, distorting, or oversimplifying it, then attacking the distorted version rather than the actual position.
example: You said we should reduce military spending. So you want to leave the country completely defenseless?
structure: Person P holds position X. Distorted version X' is presented. X' is refuted. Therefore X is false.

name: False Dichotomy
description: Presenting only two options as if they are the only possibilities when other alternatives exist. Also called false dilemma or either-or fallacy.
example: You're either with us or against us.
structure: Either A or B. Not A. Therefore B. (Ignoring alternatives C, D, etc.)

name: Slippery Slope
description: Claiming that a relatively small first step inevitably leads to a chain of negative consequences without establishing the causal links.
example: If we allow same-sex marriage, next people will want to marry their pets.
structure: A leads to B, B leads to C, C leads to D (without justification). D is bad. Therefore A is bad.

name: Hasty Generalization
description: Drawing a broad conclusion from a small or unrepresentative sample. The sample size is too small to support the generalization.
example: I met two rude people from that city — people there must be unfriendly.
structure: Small sample S has property P. Therefore all members of the population have P.

name: Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc
description: Assuming that because one event followed another, the first caused the second. Temporal sequence does not establish causation.
example: I wore my lucky socks and then we won the game. My socks caused the win.
structure: A occurred before B. Therefore A caused B.

name: Appeal to Authority
description: Treating the opinion of an authority as evidence in a domain where they have no special expertise, or appealing to authority in lieu of argument.
example: A famous actor says this diet works, so it must be effective.
structure: Authority X believes claim C. Therefore C is true.

name: Appeal to Popularity
description: Arguing that something is true or good because many people believe it or do it. Also known as argumentum ad populum or bandwagon fallacy.
example: Millions of people believe in astrology, so there must be something to it.
structure: Many people believe C. Therefore C is true.

name: Circular Reasoning
description: The conclusion is used as a premise in the argument itself, either explicitly or in a disguised form. Also called begging the question.
example: The Bible is true because it says so in the Bible.
structure: C is true because of P, where P assumes C.

name: Red Herring
description: Introducing an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the original issue. The introduced topic has no logical bearing on the conclusion.
example: Why worry about the budget deficit when there are people starving in the world?
structure: Issue A is raised. Topic B (unrelated) is introduced. Conclusion about A is drawn from B.

name: Appeal to Ignorance
description: Claiming something is true because it has not been proven false, or false because it has not been proven true. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
example: No one has ever proven that ghosts don't exist, so they must exist.
structure: There is no proof that not-C. Therefore C is true.

name: False Equivalence
description: Treating two significantly different things as if they are equivalent or comparable in relevant respects when they are not.
example: Teaching evolution and creationism is the same — both are just theories.
structure: A and B share superficial feature F. Therefore A and B are equivalent in all relevant respects.

name: Equivocation
description: Using a word in two different senses within the same argument, exploiting its ambiguity to reach a misleading conclusion.
example: Nothing is better than eternal happiness. A ham sandwich is better than nothing. Therefore a ham sandwich is better than eternal happiness.
structure: Word W means X in premise 1. W means Y in premise 2. Conclusion drawn as if W is consistent.

name: Appeal to Emotion
description: Using emotional manipulation rather than valid reasoning to persuade. Emotions are used as a substitute for logical evidence.
example: Think of the children! We can't allow this policy.
structure: Claim C evokes emotion E. Therefore C is true or should be acted upon.

name: No True Scotsman
description: Redefining a category to exclude counterexamples that would refute a generalization, rather than acknowledging the counterexample.
example: No true Scotsman would do such a thing. (After being told a Scotsman did it.) Well, no true Scotsman would.
structure: All members of group G have property P. Person Q is in G and lacks P. Redefine G to exclude Q.

name: Composition
description: Inferring that a whole has a property because its parts have that property. What is true of the parts is not necessarily true of the whole.
example: Every player on the team is talented, therefore the team is talented.
structure: All parts P of whole W have property X. Therefore W has property X.

name: Division
description: Inferring that parts must have a property because the whole has that property. The reverse of the composition fallacy.
example: The company is profitable, so each department must be profitable.
structure: Whole W has property X. Therefore each part P of W has property X.

name: Appeal to Tradition
description: Arguing that something is good or correct because it has always been done that way. Historical practice does not establish current validity.
example: We should keep this policy because it's the way we've always done things.
structure: Practice P has always been done. Therefore P is correct or should be continued.

name: Appeal to Nature
description: Claiming that something is good because it is natural, or bad because it is unnatural. The natural-artificial distinction does not map onto good-bad.
example: Herbal remedies are better than pharmaceuticals because they're natural.
structure: X is natural. Natural things are good. Therefore X is good.

name: Genetic Fallacy
description: Evaluating a claim based on its origin rather than its merits. The source of an argument is irrelevant to its logical validity.
example: That idea came from a Nazi scientist, so it must be wrong.
structure: Claim C originated from source S. S has property P (e.g., bad). Therefore C is false.

name: Loaded Question
description: Asking a question that contains a hidden assumption the respondent is forced to accept by answering. The question presupposes what it purports to ask.
example: Have you stopped cheating on your taxes?
structure: Question Q presupposes assumption A. Answering Q in any way accepts A.

name: Correlation-Causation Fallacy
description: Concluding that one thing causes another based solely on correlation. Two variables can correlate for many reasons: coincidence, common cause, or reverse causation.
example: Ice cream sales and drowning rates both rise in summer, so ice cream causes drowning.
structure: A and B are correlated. Therefore A causes B.

name: Tu Quoque
description: Deflecting a criticism by pointing out the critic's own inconsistency. Also known as appeal to hypocrisy. The critic's behavior is irrelevant to the validity of the criticism.
example: You tell me to exercise more, but you don't exercise either.
structure: Person P criticizes X. P also does X. Therefore the criticism is invalid.

name: Appeal to Force
description: Using threat of harm or coercion to make someone accept a conclusion, rather than providing a logical argument. Argumentum ad baculum.
example: You'd better agree with my position, or you'll lose your job.
structure: Accept C or face consequence H. Therefore C is true.

name: Bandwagon Fallacy
description: A variant of appeal to popularity. Arguing that one should do or believe something because everyone else is doing or believing it.
example: Everyone is investing in this stock right now — you should too.
structure: Everyone does/believes X. Therefore you should do/believe X.

name: Middle Ground Fallacy
description: Assuming that a compromise between two extremes must be correct. Also called argument to moderation or false balance. The correct position may be extreme.
example: One side says vaccines are safe, another says they're dangerous, so the truth must be somewhere in between.
structure: Position A and position B are extremes. Therefore the midpoint M is correct.

name: Cherry Picking
description: Selecting only the evidence that supports a conclusion while ignoring contradictory evidence. Also called confirmation bias in argument form.
example: Studies show X works! (Ignoring the majority of studies showing it doesn't.)
structure: Evidence E1, E2 support C. Evidence E3, E4, E5 contradict C but are omitted. Therefore C.

name: Ecological Fallacy
description: Making inferences about individuals based on aggregate data for the group they belong to.
example: Country X has a high average income, so citizen Y must be wealthy.
structure: Group G has aggregate property P. Individual I is in G. Therefore I has property P.

name: Gambler's Fallacy
description: Believing that past random events affect the probability of future independent events. Independent probabilities do not accumulate.
example: The coin has landed heads ten times in a row, so tails is now more likely.
structure: Independent event A has occurred N times. Therefore the probability of not-A on the next trial is increased.

name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
description: Continuing a course of action because of previously invested resources (time, money, effort) that cannot be recovered, rather than evaluating future prospects.
example: We've invested too much in this project to abandon it now.
structure: Resources R have already been spent on X. Therefore X should be continued.

name: Affirming the Consequent
description: Concluding the antecedent is true because the consequent is true. An invalid form of modus ponens. If P then Q; Q; therefore P — is formally invalid.
example: If it rains, the ground is wet. The ground is wet. Therefore it rained.
structure: If P then Q. Q is true. Therefore P is true. (Invalid — Q may have other causes.)

name: Denying the Antecedent
description: Concluding the consequent is false because the antecedent is false. An invalid formal argument form.
example: If you study, you'll pass. You didn't study. Therefore you won't pass.
structure: If P then Q. Not-P. Therefore not-Q. (Invalid — Q may be true for other reasons.)

name: Nirvana Fallacy
description: Comparing real-world situations to idealized, perfect alternatives, and rejecting solutions because they fall short of perfection.
example: There's no point in trying to reduce pollution unless we can eliminate it entirely.
structure: Solution S does not achieve ideal I perfectly. Therefore S should be rejected.

name: Anecdotal Evidence
description: Using personal experience or isolated examples as evidence for a general claim, ignoring statistical evidence or broader patterns.
example: My grandfather smoked all his life and lived to 95, so smoking can't be that bad.
structure: Personal case C has property P. Therefore the general claim about P holds.

name: Moving the Goalposts
description: Changing the criteria for a claim after the original criteria have been satisfied, in order to avoid conceding the point.
example: Show me one study proving X. (One is shown.) Show me five peer-reviewed studies. (Five are shown.) Show me a meta-analysis.
structure: Criterion C1 is set. C1 is met. Criterion C2 is substituted. Repeat.

name: Burden of Proof Shifting
description: Incorrectly claiming that the burden of proof lies with those who deny a claim rather than those who assert it. The burden lies with the person making the positive claim.
example: Prove that God doesn't exist.
structure: Claim C is asserted. Burden of proof placed on those denying C rather than those asserting it.

name: Appeal to Pity
description: Attempting to persuade by evoking pity rather than providing evidence. Argumentum ad misericordiam.
example: You should accept my late homework — I've been having a terrible month.
structure: Person P is in unfortunate situation S. Therefore claim C by P should be accepted.

name: Overgeneralization
description: Drawing a sweeping conclusion from insufficient evidence, applying it too broadly. Similar to hasty generalization but applies to the scope of conclusions.
example: Women are better at multitasking than men.
structure: Evidence E observed in context X. Conclusion C drawn for all contexts.

name: Special Pleading
description: Applying a general rule or standard to others but claiming an exemption for oneself or a favored case without adequate justification.
example: Lying is wrong, but it's okay when I do it to protect feelings.
structure: Rule R applies to all cases. Exception claimed for case C without principled justification.

name: Argument from Silence
description: Inferring something from the absence of evidence or statement about it. Silence about a topic does not constitute evidence for any claim about it.
example: The ancient texts never mention X, so X never existed.
structure: Source S does not mention claim C. Therefore C is false.

name: Availability Heuristic
description: Overestimating the probability of events that are easily recalled or vivid, rather than based on statistical frequency.
example: Plane crashes are frequently in the news, so flying must be more dangerous than driving.
structure: Examples of X are easily recalled. Therefore X is common or probable.

name: Modus Tollens (Valid)
description: A valid deductive argument form: if P implies Q, and Q is false, then P must be false. This is a legitimate form of reasoning, not a fallacy.
example: If it rains, the ground is wet. The ground is not wet. Therefore it did not rain.
structure: If P then Q. Not-Q. Therefore not-P. (Valid)

name: Modus Ponens (Valid)
description: A valid deductive argument form: if P implies Q, and P is true, then Q must be true. This is a legitimate form of reasoning.
example: If you study, you'll pass. You studied. Therefore you'll pass.
structure: If P then Q. P is true. Therefore Q is true. (Valid)

name: Reductio ad Absurdum (Valid)
description: A valid form of argumentation that refutes a claim by showing that it leads to an absurd or contradictory conclusion.
example: If infinite regress were acceptable, we could never explain anything. That's absurd. So infinite regress is not acceptable.
structure: Assume C. C implies absurdity A. Therefore not-C.

name: Amphiboly
description: Exploiting grammatical ambiguity in a statement to draw a misleading conclusion. The ambiguity is structural rather than in a single word.
example: The professor told the student she was wrong. (Who is wrong?)
structure: Statement S is grammatically ambiguous between meaning M1 and M2. Argument proceeds as if S has both meanings.

name: Accent Fallacy
description: Changing the meaning of a statement by emphasizing different words, or misrepresenting a position by taking it out of context.
example: We should not speak ill of our friends. (Emphasizing 'friends' implies we should speak ill of others.)
structure: Statement S with emphasis E1 has meaning M1. Interpreting S with different emphasis E2 to derive M2.

name: Existential Fallacy
description: In classical logic, drawing a conclusion about existing things from premises that only involve general statements about categories, without confirming any members exist.
example: All unicorns have horns. Some horned animals are horses. Therefore some unicorns are horses.
structure: Universal premises about category C assumed to have existing members.

name: Irrelevant Conclusion
description: An argument that purports to prove one thing but actually proves something else. Also called ignoratio elenchi — missing the point.
example: We should be lenient with this criminal — crime is a serious problem in society.
structure: Premises P support conclusion C. Conclusion C' (different from C) is presented as if following from P.

name: Texas Sharpshooter
description: Choosing a cluster of data that fits a pattern after the fact, ignoring data that doesn't fit. Named after someone who shoots at a wall then draws a target around the bullet holes.
example: These ten historical events all happened in years divisible by seven — there must be a pattern.
structure: Data D is examined. Pattern P is found in subset S. Conclusion drawn that P is meaningful, ignoring data outside S.

name: Appeal to Consequences
description: Arguing that a claim is true or false based on whether believing it has good or bad consequences, rather than on evidence.
example: If evolution is true, there is no basis for morality — so evolution must be false.
structure: If C is true, bad consequence B follows. Therefore C is false.
